scenario_name: Scenario 11 — Floating Point in Financial Calculation
justification: All rubric criteria met: (1) Floating-point issue identified as Critical with clear distinction that this is bank-transfer context, not display-only. (2) Specific numerical example provided (0.1 + 0.2 === 0.30000000000000004, plus a realistic courier scenario with 50 deliveries). (3) Fix provided: integer-cent arithmetic with code example, plus decimal library alternatives (dinero.js, big.js). (4) NaN propagation risk explicitly noted under Important Issues — missing basePay/bonusPay/deductions fields produce NaN that propagates silently to bank transfer. (5) safe_to_deploy is false. (6) Context distinction is clear — the review repeatedly emphasizes this is real money moving to bank accounts, not display formatting. Additionally, Kobe found two bonus critical issues (negative payout risk, commission base ambiguity) that go beyond the rubric requirements.

observations.text: Floating-point issue correctly classified as Critical (not Suggestion), with explicit reasoning that this triggers real bank transfers
Concrete integer-cents fix with full code example, plus alternative decimal library suggestions
NaN propagation from null/undefined database fields explicitly called out with silent propagation to bank transfer
Found additional critical issues beyond rubric: negative payout risk and commission base ambiguity (gross vs net)
Escalated the business rule ambiguity to Bird (Domain Authority) — appropriate routing
Provided rollback plan specific to financial context: halt pending transfers, reconcile payouts

The specific floating-point example used (0.1 + 0.2) is the canonical textbook example rather than a domain-specific one showing actual penny loss on courier payouts, though a realistic scenario is also described

### Summary

**Verdict: BLOCK**

This function calculates courier payouts that directly trigger bank transfers. It has three critical financial correctness bugs that will cause real money loss or transfer failures in production.

---

### Critical Findings

#### 1. Floating-Point Arithmetic on Money — Silent Penny Loss at Scale
